{{Short description|Men's association football team}}
{{About|the men's team|the women's team|Lebanon women's national football team}}
{{Good article}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=January 2019}}
{{Use British English|date=April 2020}}
<!--collective noun "team" is therefore plural--->

| Badge                 = Lebanon national football team.png

| rightarm2             = FFFFFF
| shorts2               = FFFFFF
| socks2                = FFFFFF
| FIFA Rank             = {{FIFA World Rankings|LBN}}
| FIFA max              = 77
| FIFA max date         = September 2018
| FIFA min              = 178
| FIFA min date         = April–May 2011
| Elo Rank              = {{World Football Elo Ratings|Lebanon}}
| Elo max               = 46
| Elo max date          = 27 April 1940
| Elo min               = 164
| Elo min date          = 28 July 2011
| First game            = {{nowrap|{{fb|PAL}} [[1940 Mandatory Palestine v Lebanon football match|5–1]] {{fb-rt|LBN|1920}}}}<br />{{nowrap|([[Jaffa]], [[Mandatory Palestine]]; 27 April 1940)}}
| Largest win           = {{fb|LBN|1943}} 11–1 {{fb-rt|PHI|1936}}<br />([[Tokyo]], Japan; 28 September 1967)
| Largest loss          = {{fb|IRQ|1959}} 8–0 {{fb-rt|LBN|1943}}<br />([[Baghdad]], Iraq; 25 November 1959)<br />{{fb|QAT}} 8–0 {{fb-rt|LBN|1943}}<br />([[Doha]], Qatar; 27 March 1985)
| Regional name         = [[AFC Asian Cup|Asian Cup]]
| Regional cup apps     = 3
| Regional cup first    = [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000]]
| Regional cup best     = Group stage ([[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000]], [[2019 AFC Asian Cup|2019]], [[2024 AFC Asian Cup|2023]])
| 2ndRegional name      = [[FIFA Arab Cup|Arab Cup]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 8
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[1963 Arab Cup|1963]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = Third place ([[1963 Arab Cup|1963]])
| 3rdRegional name      = [[WAFF Championship]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps  = 7
| 3rdRegional cup first = [[2000 WAFF Championship|2000]]
| 3rdRegional cup best  = Group stage ([[2000 WAFF Championship|2000]], [[2002 WAFF Championship|2002]], [[2004 WAFF Championship|2004]], [[2007 WAFF Championship|2007]], [[2012 WAFF Championship|2012]], [[2014 WAFF Championship|2014]], [[2019 WAFF Championship|2019]])
| medaltemplates        =
{{MedalSport|Men's [[Association football|football]]}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Arab Cup]]}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1963 Arab Cup|1963 Lebanon]]|}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Football at the Arab Games|Arab Games]]}}
{{MedalBronze|[[Football at the 1957 Arab Games|1957 Beirut]]|}}
{{MedalBronze|[[Football at the 1997 Arab Games|1997 Beirut]]|}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Football at the Mediterranean Games|Mediterranean Games]]}}
{{MedalBronze|[[Football at the 1959 Mediterranean Games|1959 Beirut]]|}}
| website               = {{URL|https://the-lfa.com.lb/}} {{in lang|ar}}
}}
The '''Lebanon national football team''' ({{langx|ar|منتخب لبنان لكرة القدم}}), controlled by the [[Lebanese Football Association]] (LFA), represents [[Lebanon]] in men's international [[association football]]. Founded in 1933, the LFA was one of the first football governing bodies established in the Middle East and joined [[FIFA]] in 1936. Lebanon competes as a member of the [[Asian Football Confederation]] (AFC), which it joined in 1964.

Lebanon played their first [[1940 Mandatory Palestine v Lebanon football match|FIFA-recognised international match]] against [[Mandatory Palestine national football team|Mandatory Palestine]] in 1940. They regularly participated in regional competitions such as the [[Football at the Arab Games|Arab Games]] and [[Arab Cup]] during the 1950s and 1960s, achieving third-place finishes at the [[Football at the 1957 Arab Games|1957]] and [[Football at the 1997 Arab Games|1997]] Arab Games and the [[1963 Arab Cup|1963]] Arab Cup. The [[Lebanese Civil War]] disrupted the national team's activities between 1975 and 1990, causing several withdrawals from international competitions before a gradual return to regular international football in the 1990s.

Although Lebanon have never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]], they reached the final round of AFC qualification twice, for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC fourth round|2014]] and [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|2022]] tournaments. The team has qualified for the [[AFC Asian Cup]] three times, first appearing as hosts in [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000]] and later qualifying for the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup|2019]] and [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023]] editions. At the 2019 tournament, Lebanon recorded their first Asian Cup victory and narrowly missed progression to the knockout stage on the fair play rule. They have also competed in regional tournaments such as the Arab Cup, [[WAFF Championship]], and Arab Games.

Known as "the Cedars" ({{langx|ar|رجال الأرز|links=no}}), after Lebanon's [[Cedrus libani|national symbol]], the team traditionally wears red home kits and white away kits inspired by the colours of the Lebanese flag. Lebanon reached their highest [[FIFA Men's World Ranking|FIFA World Ranking]] of 77th in September 2018, during a 16-match unbeaten run. The team's primary home venue is the [[Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium]] in [[Beirut]], although it also plays matches at other stadiums across the country.

==History==
===1933–1947: Formation and early matches===
Lebanon was one of the first nations in the [[Middle East]] to establish a formal administrative body for [[association football]].{{efn|The FA's of [[Football Federation Islamic Republic of Iran|Iran]], [[Egyptian Football Association|Egypt]], [[Turkish Football Federation|Turkey]], and [[Israel Football Association|Israel]] are older.{{sfn|Henshaw|1979|p=420}}}}{{sfn|Henshaw|1979|p=420}} On 22&nbsp;March 1933, representatives from 13 football clubs gathered in the Minet El Hosn district of [[Beirut]] to form the [[Lebanese Football Association]] (LFA).<ref name="akhbar2018">{{Cite web |last=Hawi |first=Grace |date=25 June 2009 |script-title=ar:الإعلام الرياضي في لبنان بين شباك السياسة والإهمال |trans-title=Sports media in Lebanon between politics and neglect |url=https://al-akhbar.com/Sport/135020 |access-date=20 December 2018 |publisher=[[Al Akhbar (Lebanon)|Al Akhbar]] |language=ar}}</ref><ref name="LFAabout">{{Cite web |script-title=ar:لمحة عن الإتحاد |trans-title=About the Federation |url=http://lebanesefootballassociation.com/%d8%a7%d9%84%d8%a7%d8%aa%d8%ad%d8%a7%d8%af/ |url-status=usurped |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181227151737/http://lebanesefootballassociation.com/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D8%AF/ |archive-date=27 December 2018 |access-date=20 December 2018 |publisher=[[Lebanese Football Association]] |language=ar}}</ref> The LFA was initially led by Hussein Sejaan,{{Sfn|Sakr|1992|p=17}} and became a member of [[FIFA]] in 1936.<ref name="LFAabout" /><ref name="elsportLFA">{{Cite web |date=2 March 2013 |script-title=ar:تاريخ تاسيس الاتحاد اللبناني لكرة القدم؟ |trans-title=The date of the establishment of the Lebanese Football Federation? |url=https://www.elsport.com/news/show/126050/تاريخ-تاسيس-الاتحاد-اللبناني-لكرة-القدم؟ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190405073924/https://www.elsport.com/news/show/126050/%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%AE-%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%B3%D9%8A%D8%B3-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D8%AF-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%8A-%D9%84%D9%83%D8%B1%D8%A9-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%82%D8%AF%D9%85%D8%9F |archive-date=5 April 2019 |access-date=20 December 2018 |website=Elsport |language=ar}}</ref>

On 3&nbsp;February 1934, the LFA organized a training camp for 22 players from Beirut in preparation for a [[Exhibition game#International football|friendly match]] against the Romanian club [[CA Timișoara]] (TAC).<ref name="TAC1934">{{Cite news |last=Frem |first=Joseph |date=7 February 1934 |title=A propos de la Sélection de l'Equipe de Beyrouth |trans-title=About the Beirut Team call-ups |work=[[L'Orient-Le Jour|L'Orient]] |language=fr}}</ref> The players were divided into two teams and competed against each other at the [[American University of Beirut]] (AUB) field.<ref name="TAC1934" /> However, the scheduled match against TAC on 18&nbsp;February was canceled due to financial disputes between the LFA and AUB, which had organized the event.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Frem |first=Joseph |date=15 February 1934 |title=A la F.L.F.A. |trans-title=To the F.L.F.A. [Fédération Libanaise de Football] |work=[[L'Orient-Le Jour|L'Orient]] |language=fr}}</ref> The [[Beirut XI|Beirut&nbsp;XI]] team eventually faced TAC on 21&nbsp;November 1935 at the AUB field,<ref>{{Cite news |date=30 November 1935 |title=All-Beirut vs TAC |url=https://ulib.aub.edu.lb/kullyah/capaub_al-kulliyah_rev_v1-5_1933-1937.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181221134650/https://ulib.aub.edu.lb/kullyah/capaub_al-kulliyah_rev_v1-5_1933-1937.pdf |archive-date=21 December 2018 |access-date=21 December 2018 |work=Al-Kulliyah Review |publisher=[[American University of Beirut]] |page=317 |volume=3 |issue=4}}</ref> losing 3–0.<ref>{{Cite news |date=23 November 1935 |script-title=ar:النهضة تهزم التاك والتاك يهزم منتخب بيروت |trans-title=Nahda defeats TAC and TAC defeats the Beirut team |work=[[An-Nahar]] |language=ar}}</ref>

On 29&nbsp;October 1939, the Beirut&nbsp;XI played their first match against Syria's Damascus&nbsp;XI in Beirut, resulting in a 5–4 defeat.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 31 October 1939.</ref> They played a return game in [[Damascus]] on 14&nbsp;November, which resulted in a 6–1 win for Beirut&nbsp;XI.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). January–February 1947.</ref> Over the following years, the two teams competed in 16 unofficial matches until 1963, with the Beirut&nbsp;XI securing seven victories, two draws, and seven losses.<ref name="RSSSFprehist">{{Cite web |last1=Mubarak |first1=Hassanin |last2=Morrison |first2=Neil |title=Lebanon – International Results – Early History |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesl/leb-intres-prehist.html |access-date=7 October 2018 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref>

[[File:Camille Cordahi v Mandatory Palestine, 1940.jpg|thumb|alt=Lebanese forward Camille Cordahi running downfield toward the camera|Lebanese forward [[Camille Cordahi]] during the [[1940 Mandatory Palestine v Lebanon football match|1940 match against Mandatory Palestine]]]]
The Lebanese national team's [[1940 Mandatory Palestine v Lebanon football match|first official FIFA-recognized match]] was played on 27&nbsp;April 1940, facing [[Mandatory Palestine national football team|Mandatory Palestine]] at the [[Maccabiah Stadium]] in Palestine.<ref name="results">{{Cite web |last=Mahfoud |first=Maroun |date=25 Jul 2023 |title=Lebanon – List of International Matches |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesl/leb-intres.html |access-date=26 Jul 2023 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> The game ended in a 5–1 defeat for Lebanon, with an estimated attendance of 6,000 to 10,000 spectators.<ref name="filastin">{{Cite news |date=28 April 1940 |script-title=ar:مباراة كبرى في كرة القدم بين منتخب لبنان ومنتخب فلسطين اليهودي |trans-title=A major football match between the Lebanon national team and the Jewish Palestine national team |url=https://www.nli.org.il/en/newspapers/?a=is&oid=falastin19400428-01&type=staticpdf&pdfaccesscode=PdKVQ2kxzJfgedjidIZE0ITONQWHSiMb50Mk1dRUV9WPI%3D&submitted=1&e=-------en-20--1--img-txIN%7CtxTI--------------1&g-recaptcha-response=03AFcWeA6m_zCsLiNYybAmlEFjHczvVm-so0JF3ooyhqo7mb4Wnqp-QFMqeKw_SQwGy79MY1HxYo-3ydyIcblcI3csNTAarf5XFfadCp14u38hcn-QkkR2oImUxAVdkh9RK04XDWLsZPxhekCZ-gT7zwtjTa47tD2TghZTVQloG2UZDcPui26x_dZMEos0C8FUKSc3mXEpbKMXCsscFgGks7mSf63IJpwTBi4BjVmHVz8TydfZXEC0vFEH99iZcfF1D5lG_9fARcb37cN1QJLk9DIUOiwI-6wTSmva8rygm3_9dj8WAlS9nTjb7DdNokQHTJ1QbDdagFqvvZQCCAbTE2pEuCCpfFAWOHdVrAbES_unJJSuZPZAtCEHLE94bcaEjDhxO8HhuBJdimkNVeJkPTZAfXDTJDDBKCIth5DPc0CaB2ulXCVvzNe3LWYN6Wh_4g_nY9pZUzXdWIlrigSHSFKNniVhtkxuT_A6NjDPDc2kkr6VH9sgeoYGROXbcBo1XJx6q7LDM75ltPNLgHsDKv6_1LnCEShebthhGVndDWY4orAGQ8k8mACkLHmH429vUqOWtRgNBMfq6SHEPerOTQjIJogGGA5-DupG6LrUjaY9eQOmKaJYeHcP63ZbtMxontGfEXeJm14oTkBcrzTDhAe1a37JpEOqILtS134V8Y19HtWSWyYSEksLbEZpuOyhfJNKvqKMjzu5ekF6hxZil_CLWgoM_BPWh9M5VVUY97_uQWQRluXz9W7OyqjU76G0hQgaeo_73rmFnbd0g11mm8Wt246DfVNFhdTgZRyYdbRZwOByvTmym_-Q_Jr4OGs3phem_6UGaqqmSIpuAK1PkajMko8m-B542aaZs_29uezr2t1JXvEu5TL6_O5KryVQR1J2Cmk2STj-m_nq0V7WrK3C3m0bNGsjJyAVvx2ARheIEB-bnCrKBSU |access-date=14 March 2025 |work=[[Falastin|Filastin]] |pages=4 |language=ar}}</ref> Mandatory Palestine dominated the first half, scoring four goals.<ref name="palestinepost">{{Cite news |date=30 April 1940 |title=Lebanon outclassed by Palestine selected |url=http://jpress.org.il/Olive/APA/NLI_heb/SharedView.Article.aspx?href=PLS%2F1940%2F04%2F30&id=Ar00900&sk=E28D5529 |access-date=25 March 2020 |newspaper=[[The Jerusalem Post|The Palestine Post]]}}</ref> In the second half, Lebanese forward [[Camille Cordahi]], assisted by [[Muhieddine Jaroudi]], scored Lebanon's first official international goal.<ref name="palestinepost" /> Mandatory Palestine added a fifth goal, concluding the match with a 5–1 victory.<ref name="palestinepost" />

Lebanon played their first official match against [[Syria national football team|Syria]] on 26&nbsp;April 1942, losing 2–1 in Beirut as part of the Coupe Hauteclocque.<ref name="LJ280442">{{Cite news |last=Khadra |first=A. |date=28 April 1942 |title=[...] par 2 a 1 sur Liban |trans-title=[...] 2–1 against Lebanon |work=[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]] |language=fr}}</ref> The trophy, donated by French diplomat {{Ill|Jean de Hauteclocque|fr}} in 1939, was intended to be a regular fixture between Lebanon and Syria.<ref name="LJ190342">''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 19 March 1942.</ref> The match, held at the AUB field in front of 3,000 spectators, saw Lebanon coached by [[Abed Traboulsi]] and captained by [[Labib Majdalani]].<ref name="LJ280442" /> Although two matches were initially planned,<ref name="LJ190342" /> only the Beirut fixture took place.<ref name="LJ280442" /> The Coupe Hauteclocque remained a point of contention, as the [[Syrian Football Association]] had retained possession of the cup since 1939.<ref name="LJ190342" /> Lebanon played two additional friendlies against Syria in 1947, losing 4–1 in Beirut on 4&nbsp;May,<ref>{{Cite news |date=6 May 1947 |title=Plus homogène et plus rapide que l'équipe libanaise. L'équipe syrienne gagne par 4 buts a 1 |trans-title=More consistent and faster than the Lebanese team. The Syrian team wins by 4 goals to 1 |work=[[L'Orient-Le Jour|Le Jour]] |language=fr}}</ref> and 1–0 in [[Aleppo]] on 18&nbsp;May.<ref>{{Cite news |date=20 May 1947 |title=Foot-ball: Le match-revanche Liban–Syrie. L'équipe syrienne gagne par 1 but a 0 |trans-title=Football: The Lebanon–Syria rematch. The Syrian team wins by 1 goal to 0 |work=[[L'Orient-Le Jour|Le Jour]] |language=fr}}</ref>

===1953–1963: Early international football and regional tournaments===
Coached by [[Vinzenz Dittrich]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Vinzenz Dittrich |url=http://rapidarchiv.at/spieler/dittrich_vinzenz.html |access-date=5 December 2018 |website=RapidArchiv}}</ref> Lebanon participated in their first international tournament at the [[Football at the 1953 Arab Games|inaugural edition]] of the [[Football at the Arab Games|football tournament at the Arab Games]], held in Egypt in 1953.<ref>{{Cite news |date=3 August 1953 |script-title=ar:مبارتا كرة القدم الليلة |trans-title=Two football matches tonight |work=[[Al Misri]] |language=ar}}</ref> The team finished at the bottom of its group after a 4–1 loss to [[Jordan national football team|Jordan]],<ref>{{Cite news |date=4 August 1953 |script-title=ar:ف كرة القدم: الاردن ٤ لبنان ١ |trans-title=In football: Jordan 4 Lebanon 1 |work=[[Al Misri]] |language=ar}}</ref> and a 0–0 draw with Syria.<ref>{{Cite news |date=7 August 1953 |script-title=ar:سوريا ولبنان يتعادلان |trans-title=Syria and Lebanon draw |work=[[Al Misri]] |language=ar}}</ref> In the fifth-place match, Lebanon secured a 9–1 victory over [[Palestine national football team|Palestine]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=9 August 1953 |script-title=ar:لبنان الخمسة في كرة القدم او تفوز على فلسطين ٩ – ١ |trans-title=Lebanon finish fifth in football and win against Palestine 9–1 |work=[[Al Misri]] |language=ar}}</ref>

In February 1956, Lebanon played [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]] in a notable 4–1 home defeat, during which Hungarian star [[Ferenc Puskás]] scored two goals.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1 March 1956 |script-title=ar:فاز المجريون على منتخب بيروت 4–1 |trans-title=The Hungarians defeated the Beirut team 4–1 |work=[[An-Nahar]] |pages=4 |language=ar}}</ref><ref>''[[Al-Hayat]]'' (in Arabic). 1 March 1956.</ref> Lebanon also participated in unofficial matches against prominent European clubs such as [[Dynamo Moscow]], [[SC Lokomotive Leipzig|Leipzig]], and [[FC Spartak Trnava|Spartak Trnava]] in 1957.<ref name="RSSSFprehist" /> That same year, Lebanon faced Romanian club [[FC Petrolul Ploiești|Energia Flacăra Ploiești]] in the opening game of the [[Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium|Sports City Stadium]] in Beirut, securing a 1–0 victory with a goal by [[Joseph Abou Mrad]].<ref name="CSSC">{{Cite web |title=Our History |url=https://camillechamounsportscity.com/our-history/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190103061430/https://camillechamounsportscity.com/our-history/ |archive-date=3 January 2019 |access-date=3 January 2019 |publisher=[[Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium|Camille Chamoun Sports City]] }}</ref>

In October 1957, Lebanon hosted the [[Football at the 1957 Arab Games|second edition]] of the Arab Games and were drawn with [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], Syria, and Jordan in the group stages.<ref name="PAG2" /> After two 1–1 draws against Saudi Arabia and Syria, Lebanon defeated Jordan 6–3 thanks to two braces by Joseph Abou Mrad and [[Mardik Tchaparian]], and one goal each by [[Robert Chehade]] and [[Levon Altounian]]; this placed them first in their group.<ref>{{Cite news |date=25 October 1957 |script-title=ar:السعودية فازت على سوريا ٣–١ ولبنان دخل الدور النهائي |trans-title=Saudi Arabia beat Syria 3–1 and Lebanon enter the knock-out round |work=[[Al-Hayat]] |language=ar}}</ref> In the semifinals, Lebanon lost 4–2 to [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]].<ref>''[[Al-Hayat]]'' (in Arabic). 27 October 1957.</ref> They finished in third place after [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]] withdrew from the third-place match.<ref name="PAG2" />

Lebanon hosted the third edition of the [[Football at the Mediterranean Games|Mediterranean Games]] in [[Football at the 1959 Mediterranean Games|1959]], held in Beirut in October, and were grouped with [[Italy national football B team|Italy B]] and [[Turkey national football B team|Turkey B]].{{efn|Both Italian and Turkish sides were made up of amateur players.<ref name="MED59" />}}<ref name="MED59" /> They finished last in the group, after four losses to the two European teams.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Fumarola |first=Alfonso |date=13 October 1959 |title=Successi in serie per gli azzurri a Beirut |work=[[Corriere dello Sport]] |pages=1–2 |language=it}}</ref><ref>''[[Al-Hayat]]'' (in Arabic). 15 October 1959.</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=19 October 1959 |title=Hier, en match-retour, face à l'Italie. La défense libanaise concède 5 buts |trans-title=Yesterday, in the return match, against Italy. The Lebanese defense conceded 5 goals |work=[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]] |language=fr}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=22 October 1959 |title=Malgré sa défaite hier, face à la Turquie. Réhabilitation de la Sélection libanaise |trans-title=Despite yesterday's defeat against Turkey. The Lebanese national team is back on track |work=[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]] |language=fr}}</ref> Lebanon played their first [[Football at the Summer Olympics|Olympic Games qualifiers]] in [[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics – Men's Middle East Qualifiers|1959]] between November and December. Coached by English coach [[Harry Wright (footballer, born 1909)|Harry Wright]], Lebanon were grouped with [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]] and [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]]. They played two games against Iraq, losing 3–0 at home and 8–0 away, before withdrawing from the qualifiers prior to their two matches against Turkey.<ref>''[[Al-Hayat]]'' (in Arabic). 17 November 1959.</ref><ref>''[[Al-Hayat]]'' (in Arabic). 26 November 1959.</ref>

The [[Football at the 1961 Arab Games|1961 Arab Games]], marking the third edition of the competition, were held in Morocco. The tournament featured six teams competing in a [[single round-robin]] format. Lebanon's national team participated in the event, under head coach [[Joseph Nalbandian]], recording a mixed set of results.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 17 August 1961.</ref> Lebanon won matches against Saudi Arabia (7–1) and [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]] (4–0), and suffered defeats against Morocco (1–0), the [[United Arab Republic national football team|United Arab Republic]] (UAR; 4–0), and [[Libya national football team|Libya]] (3–2). Lebanon finished in fourth place out of the six participating teams.<ref>''[[The Egyptian Gazette]]''. 7 September 1961.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 9 September 1961.</ref>

[[File:Lebanon national football team 1963.png|thumb|Lebanon at the [[1963 Arab Cup]]|alt=Eleven Lebanese football players posing for a photo prior to a football match]]
Lebanon hosted the inaugural [[Arab Cup]] in [[1963 Arab Cup|1963]], marking the first edition of the tournament.<ref name="AC63" /> The concept of an Arab Cup was initially proposed in 1957 by Lebanese journalist [[Nassif Majdalani]] and Izzat Al Turk, the Secretary General of the LFA.<ref>{{cite news |date=28 November 2021 |title=قبل انطلاقها.. ماذا يخبرنا التاريخ عن بطولة "كأس العرب"؟ |url=https://www.skynewsarabia.com/sport/1482833-%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%B7%D9%84%D8%A7%D9%82%D9%87%D8%A7-%D9%8A%D8%AE%D8%A8%D8%B1%D9%86%D8%A7-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%AE-%D8%A8%D8%B7%D9%88%D9%84%D8%A9-%D9%83%D8%A7%D9%94%D8%B3-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A8%D8%9F |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211204140749/https://www.skynewsarabia.com/sport/1482833-%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%B7%D9%84%D8%A7%D9%82%D9%87%D8%A7-%D9%8A%D8%AE%D8%A8%D8%B1%D9%86%D8%A7-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%AE-%D8%A8%D8%B7%D9%88%D9%84%D8%A9-%D9%83%D8%A7%D9%94%D8%B3-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A8%D8%9F |archive-date=4 December 2021 |access-date=4 December 2021 |work=Sky News Arabia |publisher=Saber Hussam-Eddin}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |date=28 November 2021 |title=كأس العرب: لبنان موطن الفكرة لم يحقق أي انجاز |url=https://www.france24.com/ar/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A3%D8%AE%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%85%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%85%D8%B1%D8%A9/20211128-%D9%83%D8%A3%D8%B3-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A8-%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86-%D9%85%D9%88%D8%B7%D9%86-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%81%D9%83%D8%B1%D8%A9-%D9%84%D9%85-%D9%8A%D8%AD%D9%82%D9%82-%D8%A3%D9%8A-%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%AC%D8%A7%D8%B2 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211204140745/https://www.france24.com/ar/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A3%D8%AE%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%85%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%85%D8%B1%D8%A9/20211128-%D9%83%D8%A3%D8%B3-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B9%D8%B1%D8%A8-%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86-%D9%85%D9%88%D8%B7%D9%86-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%81%D9%83%D8%B1%D8%A9-%D9%84%D9%85-%D9%8A%D8%AD%D9%82%D9%82-%D8%A3%D9%8A-%D8%A7%D9%86%D8%AC%D8%A7%D8%B2 |archive-date=4 December 2021 |access-date=4 December 2021 |work=france24 |publisher=}}</ref> In 1962, the LFA, under the leadership of its president Georges Dabbas, formally advocated for the establishment of the tournament.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 September 2005 |script-title=ar:موسوعة كرة القدم العراقية من الالف الى الياء ... بطولة كأس العرب |trans-title=Encyclopedia of Iraqi football from A to Z ... Arab Cup Championship |url=https://forum.kooora.com/f.aspx?t=883773 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210428193444/https://forum.kooora.com/f.aspx?t=883773 |archive-date=28 April 2021 |access-date=28 April 2021 |website=Kooora |language=ar}}</ref> The inaugural competition took place in Beirut between April and May 1963, featuring five participating teams.<ref name="AC63" /> Lebanon was placed in a group with Tunisia, Syria, Kuwait, and Jordan.<ref name="AC63" /> The Lebanese team began the tournament with a 6–0 victory over Kuwait, highlighted by a [[Hat-trick (association football)|hat-trick]] from Mardik Tchaparian.<ref name="orient63">{{Cite news |date=1 April 1963 |title=Inauguration officielle, hier, de la première "Coupe Arabe" |trans-title=Official inauguration, yesterday, of the first "Arab Cup" |work=[[L'Orient-Le Jour|L'Orient]] |language=fr}}</ref> Following another win against Jordan (5–0) and losses to Syria (3–2) and Tunisia (1–0), Lebanon secured a third-place finish in the tournament.<ref name="AC63" />

The [[Football at the 1963 Mediterranean Games|1963 Mediterranean Games]], the fourth edition of the event, was held in Italy in September. Lebanon was placed in Group B alongside Turkey, [[Spain national amateur football team|Spain]], [[Malta national amateur football team|Malta]], and the UAR. Notably, European teams in the tournament fielded amateur players. Lebanon's campaign began with a 1–0 loss to Spain,<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 21 September 1963.</ref> followed by a 4–0 loss to Turkey,<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 22 September 1963.</ref> and a 2–0 victory over Malta.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 24 September 1963.</ref> In their final group match against the UAR on 25&nbsp;September, the game was abandoned in the 27th minute due to a [[Waterlogging (agriculture)|waterlogged]] pitch with the score at 0–0; after a dispute over the venue for the rescheduled match, Lebanon withdrew from the competition and the UAR was awarded a 2–0 walkover victory.<ref>''[[La Stampa]]'' (in Italian). 26 September 1963.</ref>

===1964–1971: AFC affiliation and continued regional participation===
[[File:Joseph Abou Mrad vs Iraq, 1964 Arab Cup.png|thumb|Lebanon's captain, [[Joseph Abou Mrad]] (''left''), shaking hands with [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]]'s captain before their match at the [[1964 Arab Cup]]|alt=Two football players shaking hands, with two match officials in the background.]]
Lebanon played at the [[1964 Tripoli Fair Tournament]], held in [[Tripoli, Libya|Tripoli]], Libya in March; in a group with [[Sudan national football B team|Sudan B]], [[Morocco national football B team|Morocco B]], Malta B, and hosts Libya, they finished in first place with seven points.<ref name="FIFAbulletin42" /> The [[1964 Arab Cup]], the second edition of the tournament, was held in Kuwait in November. The competition featured five teams—Iraq, Libya, Kuwait, Jordan, and Lebanon—competing in a single-round robin format. Lebanon finished in fourth place, recording one win, one draw, and two defeats during the tournament.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 17 November 1964.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 20 November 1964.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 22 November 1964.</ref> In August 1964, Lebanon joined the [[Asian Football Confederation]] (AFC).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitstimes19640828-1.2.130.6|title=AFC Tells Indonesia: Pay or Be Sacked|date=28 August 1964|newspaper=[[The Straits Times]]|pages=18|access-date=3 June 2026|quote=New Zealand [...] were today admitted together with Lebanon and Kuwait as members of the AFC.}}</ref>

The [[Football at the 1965 Arab Games|1965 Arab Games]], the fourth edition of the event, took place in [[Cairo]], UAR. Lebanon was placed in Group A alongside the UAR, Palestine, Iraq, and [[Aden national football team|Aden]]. The team finished fourth in the group.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 4 September 1965.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 5 September 1965.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 7 September 1965.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 8 September 1965.</ref> In the [[1966 Arab Cup]], the third edition held in Iraq, Lebanon were drawn with Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait, and [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]] in Group A.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 31 March 1966.</ref> After three wins and a draw,<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 3 April 1966.</ref><ref>''Baghdad News''. 5 April 1966.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 6 April 1966.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 8 April 1966.</ref> they qualified to the semi-finals against Syria, where they lost 1–0.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 10 April 1966.</ref> In the third-place match, Lebanon lost 6–1 to Libya, finishing the competition in fourth place.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 13 April 1966.</ref> In November 1965, the LFA informed the AFC that Lebanon would not participate in the [[1968 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualifiers]] for the [[1968 AFC Asian Cup|1968 Asian Cup]].<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 7 November 1965.</ref>

Lebanon participated in the [[Football at the 1968 Summer Olympics – Men's Asian Qualifiers – Group 1|1968 Olympic Games qualifiers]], held in [[Tokyo]], Japan, from September to October 1967. Lebanon faced challenges early on, as the team's Hungarian coach, [[József Albert]], was unable to obtain a visa for Japan and was replaced by Joseph Nalbandian.<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 20 September 1967.</ref> Lebanon began with a 1–1 draw against [[South Vietnam national football team|South Vietnam]],<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 29 September 1967.</ref> followed by a 2–0 loss to [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]],<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 2 October 1967.</ref> and a 3–1 defeat to [[Japan national football team|Japan]].<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 4 October 1967.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 4 October 1967.</ref> However, the team secured two notable victories: an 11–1 win over the [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]] on 6&nbsp;October,<ref name="LJ071067">''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 7 October 1967.</ref> which remains Lebanon's largest margin of victory to date,<ref name="results" /> and a 5–2 win against [[Taiwan national football team|Taiwan]].<ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|L'Orient]]'' (in French). 10 October 1967.</ref><ref>''[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]]'' (in French). 10 October 1967.</ref> The match against the Philippines featured four goals by Joseph Abou Mrad and three by [[Samir Nassar (footballer)|Samir Nassar]].<ref name="LJ071067" /> Despite these wins, Lebanon finished third out of six teams and did not qualify for the Olympics.<ref name="OLY68" />

Four years later, Lebanon took part in the [[Football at the 1972 Summer Olympics – Men's Asian Qualifiers|1972 Olympic Games qualifiers]] under coach Joseph Abou Mrad. It was initially planned for a group stage in [[Tehran]], featuring [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], Syria, Lebanon, and Iraq. However, due to visa issues with Iranian authorities, FIFA restructured the group into knockout ties.<ref name="OLY72" /> Ultimately, Lebanon faced Iraq in a home-and-away series. Lebanon won the first match 1–0 in Beirut,<ref>{{Cite news |date=21 June 1971 |script-title=ar:آفو، برأسه، ادخلها واهتزت الشباك والجماهير |trans-title=Avo, with a header, puts it in and the net and crowd shake |work=[[An-Nahar]] |pages=6 |language=ar}}</ref> but lost the second 1–0 in [[Baghdad]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=15 July 1971 |script-title=ar:تصفيات الدورة الاولمبية: العراق يغلب لبنان في مباراة الرد في كرة القدم |trans-title=Olympic qualifiers: Iraq defeat Lebanon in the return leg football match |work=[[An-Nahar]] |pages=6 |language=ar}}</ref> A decisive third match was held in [[Istanbul]], Turkey, where Iraq secured a 2–1 victory, advancing to the second round of qualifiers; Lebanon was eliminated from the competition.<ref>{{Cite news |date=21 September 1971 |script-title=ar:نعم "زعبر" الحكم وقدم إلى العراق هدفًا هدية منتخبنا لعب وسيطر وكان بارعًا يستحق النصر |trans-title=Yes, the referee "cheated" and gave Iraq a goal; our team played, dominated, and was skillful and deserved the victory |work=[[An-Nahar]] |pages=6 |language=ar}}</ref>

Lebanon's first [[1972 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualifying]] campaign for the [[AFC Asian Cup|Asian Cup]] was in [[1972 AFC Asian Cup|1972]], under coach Abou Mrad.<ref name="AFCcoaches">{{Cite web |date=22 July 2012 |title=Asian Coaches Year: Lebanon |url=http://www.afcasiancup.com//ko/asian-coaches-year/29710-lebanon |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120722183916/http://www.afcasiancup.com//ko/asian-coaches-year/29710-lebanon |archive-date=22 July 2012 |access-date=5 December 2018 |publisher=[[AFC Asian Cup]]}}</ref> The Western Zone qualifiers, held in Kuwait in December 1971, began with an allocation match to determine Lebanon's group placement. Lebanon lost 3–0 to Bahrain,<ref>{{Cite news |date=12 December 1971 |title=Bahrain routs Ledanon {{sic}} 3–0: Asian Cup |work=[[Kuwait Times]] |pages=2}}</ref> and was placed in Group B alongside Kuwait and Syria.<ref>{{Cite news |date=13 December 1971 |title=ASIAN CUP WESTERN II REGIONAL TOURNAMENT 1971 |work=[[Kuwait Times]]}}</ref> In the group stage, Lebanon suffered a 1–0 defeat to Kuwait,<ref>{{Cite news |date=15 December 1971 |title=Lebanese team walks out |work=[[Kuwait Times]]}}</ref> but secured a 3–2 victory over Syria, advancing to the semi-finals.<ref>{{Cite news |date=20 December 1971 |title=Asian Cup: Kuwait Tops In Group I |work=[[Kuwait Times]]}}</ref> However, in the decisive semi-final match, where a win would have guaranteed qualification, Lebanon lost 4–1 to Iraq.<ref>{{Cite news |date=23 December 1975 |title=Kuwait, Iraq to play in final Friday |work=[[Kuwait Times]]}}</ref> The team concluded their campaign with a 2–0 consolation victory over Jordan in the third-place match.<ref>{{Cite news |date=25 December 1971 |title=Iraq Wins Asian Cup 1–0 |work=[[Kuwait Times]]}}</ref>

===1975–1988: Civil war and international disruptions===
[[File:Devastated Stadium - 13371618444.jpg|thumb|The destroyed [[Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium]] during the [[Lebanese Civil War]] in 1982|alt=Exterior of the destroyed Camille Chamoun stadium]]
Despite the [[Lebanese Civil War]] (1975–1990), the national football team continued participating in international competitions, though with significant disruptions. Ahead of [[1976 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualification]] for the [[1976 AFC Asian Cup|1976 Asian Cup]], in which Lebanon had originally agreed to host their group, the team withdrew due to the civil war.<ref>{{Cite news |date=25 November 1984 |title=From humble beginnings... |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/singmonitor19841125-1.2.27.3 |access-date=3 June 2026 |work=[[The Singapore Monitor]] |pages=18 |quote=Lebanon, which had originally agreed to host the group tournament also backed out because of domestic problems.}}</ref>

A notable incident occurred in 1979 during preparations for a match against South Korea, when Joseph Nalbandian, secretary-general of the LFA, was detained for six hours in the basement of the [[Kataeb Regulatory Forces]]' War Council building, the paramilitary wing of the [[Kataeb Party]].<ref name="travel1984">{{Cite news |date=21 October 1984 |script-title=ar:إتحاد الكرة يستنكر أسلوب منع المنتخب من السفر |trans-title=The Football Association condemns the method of preventing the national team from traveling |work=[[As-Safir]] |pages=8 |language=ar}}</ref> Despite this political interference, the national team proceeded with its travel to South Korea for the match.<ref name="travel1984" />

The team faced further challenges during the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup qualification|1980 Asian Cup qualifiers]], held in Abu Dhabi in November 1979. After a 0–0 draw against the [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]] (UAE), Lebanon needed a victory over Syria to secure qualification for the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup|final tournament]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=19 November 1979 |script-title=ar:شبارو يصد ضربة جزاء فتنتهي المباراة بتعادل لبنان والامارات من دون أهداف |trans-title=Chebaro saves a penalty kick, and the match ends with a goalless draw between Lebanon and the UAE |work=[[Al Anwar (Lebanese newspaper)|Al Anwar]] |pages=10 |language=ar}}</ref> The decisive match proved controversial, with refereeing decisions including a disputed [[Penalty kick (association football)|penalty]] awarded to Syria, a disallowed Lebanese goal, and the [[Ejection (sports)|sending off]] of three Lebanese players. These incidents contributed to Lebanon's 1–0 defeat, resulting in their elimination from the competition.<ref name="SYR79">{{Cite news |date=29 November 1979 |script-title=ar:الحكم يصفر ضربة جزاء على لبنان! ويلغي للمنتخب الوطني هدفاً صحيحاً! ويطرد ثلاثة لاعبين لاسباب مجهولة |trans-title=The referee whistles a penalty kick against Lebanon! He disallows a legitimate goal for the national team! And sends off three players for unknown reasons |work=[[Al Anwar (Lebanese newspaper)|Al Anwar]] |language=ar}}</ref>

Further disruptions followed in the mid-1980s Asian Cup and World Cup qualification cycles. Lebanon withdrew from the [[1984 AFC Asian Cup qualification|1984 Asian Cup qualifiers]] despite being scheduled to compete in Saudi Arabia in October 1984,<ref name="ANC84" /> after being unable to depart Beirut due to security concerns, with the LFA citing "reasons beyond its control".<ref name="travel1984" /> Similarly, in the team's first attempt at [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|qualifying]] for the [[FIFA World Cup]] in [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986]], Lebanon played four matches in March 1985 against Iraq and Qatar before withdrawing due to the escalation of the civil war,<ref name="WC86" /> with FIFA later annulling all results.<ref>{{Cite news |date=4 April 1985 |title=Le Liban se retire de la Coupe du Monde |trans-title=Lebanon withdraw from the World Cup |work=[[L'Orient–Le Jour|Le Jour]] |pages=10 |language=fr}}</ref>

At the [[Football at the 1987 Mediterranean Games|1987 Mediterranean Games]] in [[Latakia]], Syria, Lebanon participated in the football tournament but struggled competitively. After an initial 0–0 draw against [[San Marino national football team|San Marino]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 September 1987 |title=Lebanon vs San Marino |url=https://eu-football.info/_match.php?id=8707 |access-date=22 March 2025 |website=eu-football.info}}</ref> defeats to [[Turkey Olympic football team|Turkey Olympic]] (1–0)<ref>''[[Milliyet]]'' (in Turkish). 19 September 1987 ([https://gazetearsivi.milliyet.com.tr/liste?tarih=1987.09.19 link]).</ref> and hosts Syria (6–1) saw the team finish third in their group and fail to progress beyond the group stage.<ref name="MED87" />

Lebanon's final major tournament appearance during the civil war came at the [[1988 Arab Cup]]. After [[1988 Arab Cup qualification|qualifying]] through matches in Aleppo, including a 2–1 loss to Syria<ref>{{Cite news |date=12 April 1988 |script-title=ar:سوريا فازت على لبنان ٢–١ |trans-title=Syria defeats Lebanon 2–1 |work=[[An-Nahar]] |language=ar}}</ref> and a 0–0 draw with Palestine,<ref>{{Cite news |date=14 April 1988 |script-title=ar:تعادل لبنان وفلسطين ٠–٠ |trans-title=Lebanon and Palestine draw 0–0 |work=[[An-Nahar]] |language=ar}}</ref> Lebanon competed in the finals in Jordan. Drawn alongside [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]], Iraq, Tunisia, and Saudi Arabia, the team recorded one win, two draws, and one defeat, finishing third in the group and failing to advance to the knockout stages.<ref name="AC88" />

===1993–2004: Post-war revival and 2000 Asian Cup hosting===
Lebanon returned to international competition in 1993, entering the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|qualifiers]] for the [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994 World Cup]] under coach [[Adnan Al Sharqi]].<ref name="coaches">{{Cite web |last1=Mahfoud |first1=Maroun |date=12 July 2025 |title=Lebanon National Team Coaches |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/leb-coach-triv.html |access-date=29 May 2026 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> Their 57-year gap between joining FIFA in 1936 and entering a full World Cup qualifying campaign in 1993 was the longest at the time; the record was surpassed by the Philippines three years later with a gap of 68 years.<ref>{{Cite web|date=28 June 2011|title=Statistical Kit: Preliminary Draw for the 2014 FIFA World Cup Brazil|url=https://digitalhub.fifa.com/m/52463a2cc01d135/original/c91tgtfetbsmneex1vbc-pdf.pdf|access-date=21 December 2020|publisher=[[FIFA]]|page=6}}</ref> Lebanon finished third in their group with two wins, four draws, and two losses, failing to advance.<ref name="WC94" />

Under [[Terry Yorath]], Lebanon's first foreign manager after the civil war, the team began their [[1996 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualification campaign]] for the [[1996 AFC Asian Cup|1996 Asian Cup]].<ref name="ANC96" /> Despite two wins against [[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]] and only one defeat, against Kuwait, Lebanon were eliminated by a single point.<ref name="ANC96" /> During Yorath's tenure between 1995 and 1997, Lebanon rose 10 places in the [[FIFA Men's World Ranking|FIFA rankings]] following a 3–3 draw with the [[Czech Republic national football team|Czech Republic]] and a 1–0 win over Jordan in February 1997.<ref name="AFCTOTM97">{{cite web|date=February 1997|title=Thai hero Piyapong takes award|url=http://www.asian-football.com/afc/pub/april97/frame.r2.html|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/19970708000554/http://www.asian-football.com/afc/pub/april97/frame.r2.html|archive-date=8 July 1997|access-date=16 August 2020|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]}}</ref> Lebanon were also named Asian Team of the Month.<ref name="AFCTOTM97" /> In [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|qualification]] for the [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998 World Cup]], Lebanon were drawn with Kuwait and [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]],<ref name="WC98" /> and finished bottom of the group with four points.<ref name="WC98" /> Despite the elimination, Yorath remains one of Lebanon's most successful managers, winning 15 of his 31 official matches in charge.<ref name="coaches" />

{{football squad on pitch|align=right|image=striped
| GK      = '''[[Ahmad Sakr|Sakr]]'''
| RB      = '''[[Marcílio (footballer, born 1976)|Marcílio]]'''
| RCB     = '''[[Jadir Morgenstern|Jadir]]'''
| LCB     = '''[[Youssef Mohamad (footballer, born 1980)|Mohamad]]'''
| LB      = '''[[Faisal Antar|F. Antar]]'''
| DM      = '''[[Jamal Taha|Taha]] ([[Captain (association football)|c]])'''
| RCM     = '''[[Michael Reda|Reda]]'''
| LCM     = '''[[Roda Antar|R. Antar]]'''
| SS      = '''[[Luís Fernandes (footballer)|Fernandes]]'''
| RCF     = '''[[Gilberto (footballer, born 1975)|Gilberto]]'''
| LCF     = '''[[Newton (footballer, born 1976)|Newton]]'''
| caption = Lebanon's starting line-up against [[Iran national football team|Iran]] at the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000 Asian Cup]]
}}
Lebanon hosted the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000 Asian Cup]] despite FIFA concerns over stadium conditions.<ref name="guardian2000AFC">{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2000/oct/15/newsstory.sport12|title=Lebanon's Asian odyssey|date=15 October 2000|publisher=[[The Guardian]]|access-date=18 November 2018}}</ref> Coached by [[Josip Skoblar]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.rsssf.org/intldetails/2000as.html|title=International Matches 2000 – Asia|last=Courtney|first=Barrie|website=[[RSSSF]]|access-date=23 June 2019}}</ref> and captained by [[Jamal Taha]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.kooora.com/?n=768812|last=Khaled|first=Nasser|date=22 December 2018|website=Kooora|script-title=ar:جمال طه: حظوظ لبنان وافرة لتخطي دور المجموعات بكأس آسيا|trans-title=Jamal Taha: Lebanon has ample chances to overcome the group stage in the Asian Cup|access-date=23 June 2019}}</ref> Lebanon were drawn with [[Iran national football team|Iran]], Iraq, and [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] in [[2000 AFC Asian Cup Group A|Group A]].<ref name="ANC00" /> Five members of the 23-man squad were Brazilian-born players of [[Lebanese Brazilians|Lebanese descent]].<ref name="TRTdiaspora">{{Cite web|last=McIntyre|first=Scott|author-link=Scott McIntyre (journalist)|date=17 January 2019|title=How diaspora footballers came together under the Lebanese flag|url=https://www.trtworld.com/magazine/how-diaspora-footballers-came-together-under-the-lebanese-flag-23404|access-date=25 June 2021|publisher=[[TRT World]]}}</ref> Lebanon opened the tournament on 12&nbsp;October, with a 4–0 defeat to Iran at the Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium in front of 52,418 spectators.<ref name="ANC00" /> In the following match against Iraq, Lebanon recovered from a two-goal deficit to draw 2–2 through goals from [[Abbas Chahrour]]<ref>{{Cite web|date=21 September 2020|title=Bracket Challenge – Final: Abbas Chahrour (LBN) v Widodo Putra (IDN)|url=https://www.the-afc.com/competitions/afc-asian-cup/news/bracket-challenge-final-abbas-widodo|access-date=21 December 2020|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]}}</ref> and [[Moussa Hojeij]], earning their first point in the competition.<ref name="ANC00" /> A 1–1 draw against Thailand in the final group match, with [[Luís Fernandes (footballer)|Luís Fernandes]] scoring Lebanon's equaliser, was not enough to advance, as they finished bottom of the group with two points.<ref name="ANC00" />

Managed by [[Theo Bücker]], Lebanon competed in the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|first round of qualification]] for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]] against [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]], [[Sri Lanka national football team|Sri Lanka]], and Thailand.<ref name="WC02" /> Led offensively by [[Roda Antar]], [[Haitham Zein]], [[Vardan Ghazaryan]], and [[Gilberto (footballer, born 1975)|Gilberto]], Lebanon finished second in their group after scoring 26 goals in six matches. Despite finishing the qualifiers with the most goals scored in their group, Lebanon finished second and were eliminated.<ref name="WC02" />

Under [[Richard Tardy]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.todayonline.com/sports/football/fas-appoints-richard-tardy-head-coach-national-youth-teams|title=Wanted: Thinking footballers for the Lions|last=De Cotta|first=Ian|date=21 July 2015|publisher=[[TODAYOnline]]|access-date=10 December 2018}}</ref> Lebanon entered the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualifiers]] for the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup|2004 Asian Cup]].<ref name="ANC04" /> Before their away match against North Korea, the Lebanese delegation reportedly faced poor accommodation and inadequate training conditions, with their training field allegedly containing goats and sheep.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/articles/2003/10/30/1067233325247.html?from=storyrhs|title=Buddy makes name for himself in Lebanon|date=31 October 2003|work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref> Lebanon finished third in their qualifying group with four points.<ref name="ANC04" /> In the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round of qualification]] for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]], Lebanon were drawn with South Korea, [[Vietnam national football team|Vietnam]], and the [[Maldives national football team|Maldives]].<ref name="WC06" /> Coached by [[Mahmoud Hammoud (footballer)|Mahmoud Hamoud]], they finished second in the group and were eliminated.<ref name="WC06" />

===2006–2014: First World Cup qualifying final round appearance and match-fixing scandal===
Lebanon were drawn with [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], Bahrain, and Kuwait in the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2007 Asian Cup qualifiers]].<ref name="ANC07" /> The scheduled encounter against Australia led Australian-born defender [[Buddy Farah]] to return to the Lebanese national team.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.smh.com.au/news/football/buddy-hopes-we-can-be-friends/2006/01/05/1136387572920.html|title=Buddy hopes we can be friends|date=6 January 2006|work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref> However, before Lebanon's match against Bahrain on 16&nbsp;August 2006, the AFC accepted the LFA's withdrawal request due to the [[2006 Lebanon War]], which had forced several players to flee their homes.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/middle_east/5234058.stm|title=Lebanon withdraw from Asian Cup|date=1 August 2006|work=[[BBC News]]|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref>

In the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|first round of qualification]] for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]], Lebanon defeated [[India men's national football team|India]] 6–3 on aggregate to advance to the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round]].<ref name="WC10qualIND">{{Cite web|url=http://www.fifa.com/worldcup/archive/southafrica2010/preliminarydraw/news/newsid=626051/index.html|title=Lebanon advance to Round 3|date=31 October 2007|publisher=[[FIFA]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130123123828/http://www.fifa.com/worldcup/archive/southafrica2010/preliminarydraw/news/newsid=626051/index.html|archive-date=23 January 2013|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref> Drawn with Saudi Arabia, Singapore, and [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]], they finished bottom of the group without a point.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://int.soccerway.com/international/asia/wc-qualifying-asia/2010-south-africa/round-3/group-4/g1741/|title=2010 WC Qualification Asia – 3rd Round|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> In the [[2011 AFC Asian Cup qualification – first round|preliminary round]] of qualifying for the [[2011 AFC Asian Cup|2011 Asian Cup]],{{efn|[[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]], [[Myanmar national football team|Myanmar]], and [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], respectively the lowest, third-lowest, and fourth-lowest-ranked teams in Asia,<ref name="TOM" /> did not take part in the preliminary round on account of having participated in the [[2008 AFC Challenge Cup|2008]] and [[2010 AFC Challenge Cup]], which acted as qualifying tournaments to the [[2011 AFC Asian Cup]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=AFC Asian Cup 2011 and AFC Challenge Cup 2008: AFC announces seedings and revised qualification process|url=https://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_164921586.html|date=19 December 2007|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071223025109/https://www.the-afc.com/eng/articles/viewArticle.jsp_164921586.html|archive-date=23 December 2007|access-date=8 May 2020}}</ref> Only the Maldives and Lebanon, respectively the second-lowest and fifth-lowest ranked teams, were involved in the preliminary round.<ref name="ANC11" />}}<ref name="TOM">{{Cite web|url=https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20071207/sport/australia-japan-top-seeds-for-2011-asian-cup-draw.187364|title=Australia, Japan top seeds for 2011 Asian Cup draw|date=7 December 2007|publisher=[[Times of Malta]]|access-date=18 December 2018}}</ref> Lebanon defeated the Maldives 6–1 on aggregate to reach the qualifying round,<ref name="AFC11qualMDVAFC">{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/en/component/joomleague/?view=report&compID=242&matchId=1405|title=Maldives 1–2 Lebanon|date=23 April 2008|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090627071305/http://www.the-afc.com/en/component/joomleague/?view=report&compID=242&matchId=1405|archive-date=27 June 2009|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref><ref name="AFC11qualMDVFIFA">{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/ranking/news/newsid=772434.html|title=Lebanon make winning leap|publisher=[[FIFA]]|date=22 May 2008|access-date=15 August 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110906161658/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/ranking/news/newsid%3D772434.html|archive-date=6 September 2011|url-status=dead}}</ref> but finished last in a group containing [[China national football team|China]], Syria, and Vietnam.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/asian-cup-qualification/2011-qatar/qualifying-round/group-d/g2275/|title=2011 Asian Cup Qualification|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref>

[[Emile Rustom]], reappointed head coach in 2008, led Lebanon into the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round of qualification]] for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014 World Cup]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=5 November 2008|script-title=ar:الاتحاد اللبناني يعين أميل رستم مديرا فنيا لمنتخب كرة القدم|trans-title=The Lebanese Federation appoints Emile Rustom as technical director of the football team|url=https://www.radiosawa.com/archive/2008/11/05/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%AD%D8%A7%D8%AF-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%8A-%D9%8A%D8%B9%D9%8A%D9%86-%D8%A3%D9%85%D9%8A%D9%84-%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%85-%D9%85%D8%AF%D9%8A%D8%B1%D8%A7-%D9%81%D9%86%D9%8A%D8%A7-%D9%84%D9%85%D9%86%D8%AA%D8%AE%D8%A8-%D9%83%D8%B1%D8%A9-%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%82%D8%AF%D9%85|access-date=23 November 2020|publisher=[[Radio Sawa]]}}</ref> Lebanon defeated [[Bangladesh national football team|Bangladesh]] 4–2 on aggregate,<ref name="WCqual14BAN">{{Cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/preliminaries/asia/matches/round=257787/match=300165147/report.html|title=2014 FIFA World Cup Brazil™: Bangladesh – Lebanon|date=28 July 2011|publisher=[[FIFA]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121112054107/https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/preliminaries/asia/matches/round=257787/match=300165147/report.html|archive-date=12 November 2012|access-date=23 March 2020}}</ref> before being drawn with South Korea, Kuwait, and the UAE in the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round]].<ref name="SWWCqual14">{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/wc-qualifying-asia/2014-brazil/3rd-round/r14206/|title=2014 WC Qualification Asia – 3rd Round|website=Soccerway|access-date=24 March 2020}}</ref> Rustom resigned shortly after the draw, citing internal administrative issues,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dailystar.com.lb//Sports/Football/2011/Aug-03/145245-lebanons-national-football-team-coach-rustom-resigns.ashx|title=Lebanon's national football team coach Rustom resigns|date=3 August 2011|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Lebanon)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=23 March 2020|archive-date=28 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211028041015/https://www.dailystar.com.lb/Sports/Football/2011/Aug-03/145245-lebanons-national-football-team-coach-rustom-resigns.ashx|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.dailystar.com.lb//Sports/Football/2011/Aug-06/145505-a-new-coach-and-a-new-philosophy.ashx#axzz1VTbPSFoq|title=A new coach and a new philosophy|last=Laurie|first=Kenny|date=6 August 2011|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Lebanon)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=23 March 2020|archive-date=28 October 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211028045207/https://www.dailystar.com.lb/Sports/Football/2011/Aug-06/145505-a-new-coach-and-a-new-philosophy.ashx#axzz1VTbPSFoq|url-status=dead}}</ref> and Theo Bücker was reappointed as head coach in August 2011.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://arabia.eurosport.com/article/2895667-%D8%A8%D9%88%D9%83%D9%8A%D8%B1-%D9%85%D8%AF%D8%B1%D8%A8%D8%A7-%D9%84%D9%85%D9%86%D8%AA%D8%AE%D8%A8-%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86|date=4 August 2011|publisher=[[Eurosport|Eurosport Arabia]]|script-title=ar:بوكير مدربا لمنتخب لبنان|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181210154632/https://arabia.eurosport.com/article/2895667-%D8%A8%D9%88%D9%83%D9%8A%D8%B1-%D9%85%D8%AF%D8%B1%D8%A8%D8%A7-%D9%84%D9%85%D9%86%D8%AA%D8%AE%D8%A8-%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86|archive-date=10 December 2018|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref>

After opening the campaign with a 6–0 defeat to South Korea, Lebanon came from behind to beat the UAE 3–1 at home,<ref>{{Cite web|last=Al Gizouli|first=Hisham|date=7 September 2011|title=UAE's World Cup hopes in tatters|url=https://www.khaleejtimes.com/article/20110906/ARTICLE/309069903/1052|access-date=23 March 2020|publisher=[[Khaleej Times]]|archive-date=5 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605010147/https://www.khaleejtimes.com/article/20110906/ARTICLE/309069903/1052|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|date=6 September 2011|title=Lebanon vs. UAE|url=https://int.soccerway.com/matches/2011/09/06/world/wc-qualifying-asia/lebanon/united-arab-emirates/1177906/|access-date=23 March 2020|website=Soccerway}}</ref> followed by a 2–2 draw against Kuwait.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/en/tournaments/men-a-youth/fifa-world-cup/36743-lebanon-v-kuwait|title=Lebanon 2–2 Kuwait|date=11 October 2011|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120917152522/http://www.the-afc.com/en/tournaments/men-a-youth/fifa-world-cup/36743-lebanon-v-kuwait|archive-date=17 September 2012|access-date=23 March 2020}}</ref> More than 30,000 spectators attended the Kuwait match at Camille Chamoun Sports City Stadium, the first time fans had been officially allowed in the venue since 2005 due to previous crowd issues.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/no-fans-allowed-at-lebanese-soccer-games/|title=Sectarian Violence Makes Getting in to Lebanese Soccer Games a Real Bitch|last=Neumann|first=Jeff|date=1 March 2012|publisher=[[Vice Media]]|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> Fan misconduct again interrupted the match several times.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.dailystar.com.lb/Sports/Football/2011/Oct-11/151031-lebanon-draws-2-2-with-kuwait.ashx#axzz1aZ4nEXLr|title=Lebanon draws 2–2 with Kuwait in World Cup qualifier|last=Laurie|first=Kenny|date=11 October 2011|publisher=[[The Daily Star (Lebanon)|The Daily Star]]|access-date=23 March 2020}}</ref> Lebanon later defeated Kuwait 1–0 away from home, their first victory over Kuwait in [[Kuwait City]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.live-result.com/football/matches/match203220_Kuwait-Lebanon-online/|title=Kuwait 0–1 Lebanon|date=11 November 2011|website=live-result.com|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.eloratings.net/Kuwait|title=World Football Elo Ratings – Kuwait|website=eloratings.net|access-date=23 June 2019}}</ref> On 15&nbsp;November 2011, Lebanon defeated South Korea 2–1 in Beirut in front of more than 40,000 spectators.<ref name="spectators">{{Cite web |date=15 November 2011 |title=Korea stunned by Lebanon 1–2 in World Cup qualification |url=https://www.koreatimes.co.kr/sports/20111116/korea-stunned-by-lebanon-1-2-in-world-cup-qualification |access-date=28 January 2026 |publisher=[[The Korea Times]]}}</ref> Goals from [[Ali Al Saadi]] and [[Abbas Ali Atwi]] gave Lebanon their first-ever win against South Korea and secured qualification to the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC fourth round|final round of World Cup qualifying]] for the first time.<ref name="BRSK14">{{Cite web|url=https://bleacherreport.com/articles/997263-lebanon-beats-south-korea-and-writes-history-during-2014-world-cup-qualification|title=Lebanon Beats South Korea and Writes History During 2014 World Cup Qualification|last=Zahed|first=Sami|date=25 December 2011|publisher=[[Bleacher Report]]|access-date=23 June 2019}}</ref>

[[File:Iran v Lebanon, 11 June 2013 05.jpg|thumb|Lebanon against [[Iran national football team|Iran]] at the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC fourth round|2014 World Cup qualification]]|alt=A Lebanese defender slide tackling an Iranian forward]]
Lebanon were drawn with South Korea, Uzbekistan, Iran, and [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]] in the fourth round.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/wc-qualifying-asia/2014-brazil/4th-round/r14207/|title=2014 WC Qualification Asia – 4th Round|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> A 1–0 home win against Iran on 11&nbsp;September 2012, courtesy of a header by captain Roda Antar, kept Lebanon in contention for qualification.<ref name="SWWCqual14IRN">{{Cite web|title=Lebanon vs. Iran|url=https://us.soccerway.com/matches/2012/09/11/asia/wc-qualifying-asia/lebanon/iran/1269282/?ICID=PL_MS_05|date=11 September 2012|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> However, during the campaign, national team players [[Ramez Dayoub]] and [[Mahmoud El Ali]] were implicated in the [[2013 Lebanese football match-fixing scandal|2013 Lebanese match-fixing scandal]] and received lifetime bans from the Lebanese Football Association.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Crossman|first=Steve|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/21718034|title=Lebanon head coach reveals anger over match fixing|date=8 March 2013|work=[[BBC Sport]]|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.lbcgroup.tv/news/76470/lebanon-national-football-team-players-mahmoud-al|title=Lebanon National Football team players Mahmoud al-Ali, Ramez Dyoub banned for life, fined USD 15,000 for manipulating results|date=26 February 2013|publisher=[[Lebanese Broadcasting Corporation International|LBCI Lebanon]]|access-date=23 May 2017}}</ref> Lebanon's 1–0 defeat to Qatar was part of the scandal, with defender Dayoub purposely passing the ball to the Qatari striker who netted the lone goal of the game.<ref>{{cite web|title=BBC Sport – Lebanon head coach reveals anger over match fixing|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/21718034|last=Crossman|first=Steve|date=8 March 2013|work=[[BBC News]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130311183642/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/21718034|archive-date=11 March 2013|access-date=15 June 2013}}</ref> Lebanon remained in contention until the penultimate matchday, when South Korea scored a 97th-minute equaliser in Beirut to eliminate them from qualification.<ref name="SNetSK18">{{Cite web|url=https://www.sportsnet.ca/soccer/south-korea-rallies-late-to-draw-lebanon/|title=South Korea rallies late to draw Lebanon|date=4 June 2013|publisher=[[Sportsnet]]|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref>

In [[2015 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualification]] for the [[2015 Asian Cup]], Lebanon were drawn with Iran, Thailand, and Kuwait.<ref name="SWAFCqual15">{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/asian-cup-qualification/2015-australia/group-stage/r19485/|title=2015 Asian Cup Qualification|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> [[Giuseppe Giannini]] replaced Bücker as head coach during the campaign.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Barclay|first=Simon|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lztDCQAAQBAJ&q=giuseppe+giannini+lebanon+2015&pg=PA44|title=The AFC Asian Cup 2015|date=21 March 2015|publisher=[[Lulu.com]]|isbn=978-1-326-17085-1|pages=44}}</ref> Lebanon finished third in their group with two wins, two draws, and two defeats, narrowly missing qualification after China advanced as the better third-placed team on goal difference.<ref name="SWAFCqual15" /> In September 2014, Lebanon drew 2–2 with the [[Brazil national under-23 football team|Brazil Olympic team]] in an unofficial FIFA match in [[Doha]], with [[Hassan Maatouk]] having a goal incorrectly ruled offside, and Brazil scoring a goal which was erroneously ruled onside.<ref name="akhbarBrazil">{{Cite web|url=https://al-akhbar.com/Sport/37620|last=Saad|first=Abdelkader|date=9 September 2014|publisher=[[Al Akhbar (Lebanon)|Al Akhbar]]|language=ar|script-title=ar:منتخب لبنان يُحرج البرازيليين: ألمانيا ليست أفضل منّا|trans-title=The Lebanese national team embarrasses the Brazilians: Germany is no better than us|access-date=3 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://janoubia.com/2014/09/09/%d9%84%d8%a8%d9%86%d8%a7%d9%86-%d9%8a%d8%b9%d8%a7%d8%af%d9%84-%d8%a7%d9%84%d8%a8%d8%b1%d8%a7%d8%b2%d9%8a%d9%84-2-2/|date=2014-09-09|publisher=Janoubia|language=ar|script-title=ar:لبنان يعادل البرازيل 2–2|trans-title=Lebanon draw 2–2 against Brazil|access-date=3 January 2019 |last1=Barakat |first1=Ali }}</ref>  Following a 5–0 friendly defeat to Qatar the following month,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/matches/2014/10/09/world/friendlies/qatar/lebanon/1949305/|title=Qatar vs. Lebanon|date=9 October 2014|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> Giannini was dismissed.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://english.ahram.org.eg/NewsContent/6/55/129704/Sports/World/Qatar-appoint-Uruguayan-Carreno-as-new-head-coach.aspx|title=Qatar appoint Uruguayan Carreno as new head coach|date=8 May 2015|publisher=[[Al-Ahram]]|access-date=6 July 2019}}</ref>

===2015–2024: First Asian Cup qualifications and second World Cup qualifying final round appearance===
[[Miodrag Radulović]] was appointed the team's new coach in 2015,<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/lebanon-asian-cup-2019-2018-world-cup/radulovic-to-lead-lebanon%E2%80%99s-qualification-charge|title=Radulovic to lead Lebanon's qualification charge|date=8 May 2015|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170830165435/http://www.the-afc.com/lebanon-asian-cup-2019-2018-world-cup/radulovic-to-lead-lebanon%E2%80%99s-qualification-charge|archive-date=30 August 2017|access-date=10 December 2018}}</ref> and led Lebanon in the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2018 World Cup qualifications]], played between June 2015 and March 2016.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Lomas|first=Mark|url=http://www.arabnews.com/node/1360536/sport|title=Lebanon coach Miodrag Radulovic ready to take the Cedars to new heights|date=22 August 2018|work=[[Arab News]]|access-date=7 November 2018}}</ref> Drawn with South Korea, Kuwait, [[Myanmar national football team|Myanmar]], and [[Laos national football team|Laos]],<ref name="SWWCqual18">{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/wc-qualifying-asia/2018-russia/2nd-round/group-g/g8599/|title=2018 WC Qualification Asia – 2nd Round|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> Lebanon finished second in their group and advanced to the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|third round of qualification]] for the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup|2019 Asian Cup]].<ref name="roosqual19">{{Cite web|url=https://www.socceroos.com.au/news/how-lebanon-qualified-2019-afc-asian-cup|title=How Lebanon qualified for the 2019 AFC Asian Cup|last=Windon|first=Jacob|date=2 September 2018|publisher=[[Socceroos]]|access-date=10 December 2018}}</ref> Placed in a group with North Korea, [[Hong Kong national football team|Hong Kong]], and [[Malaysia national football team|Malaysia]],<ref name="SWAFCqual19">{{Cite web|url=https://us.soccerway.com/international/asia/asian-cup-qualification/2019/3rd-round/group-b/g9662/|title=2019 Asian Cup Qualification|website=Soccerway|access-date=9 December 2018}}</ref> Lebanon topped the group with five wins and a draw to qualify for the Asian Cup for the first time through qualification.{{Efn|Lebanon's first participation was in the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup|2000 edition]], which they hosted.}}<ref name="FIFAradu17">{{Cite news|url=https://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/news/y=2017/m=12/news=radulovic-s-lebanon-continue-to-climb-2924613.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171215063526/http://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/news/y=2017/m=12/news=radulovic-s-lebanon-continue-to-climb-2924613.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=15 December 2017|title=Radulovic's Lebanon continue to climb|date=14 December 2017|publisher=[[FIFA]]|access-date=11 October 2018}}</ref> Captain Hassan Maatouk<ref>{{Cite news|last=Williams|first=Paul|url=http://www.arabnews.com/node/1191601/sports|title="Focus on the future" Maatouk tells team|date=11 November 2017|work=[[Arab News]]|access-date=8 October 2018}}</ref> played a key role in the campaign, scoring five goals in six matches.<ref name="roosqual19" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Mamrud |first=Roberto |date=17 March 2024 |title=Hassan Ali Maatouk – Goals in International Matches |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/leb-maatouk-intlg.html |access-date=13 June 2024 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> Although Radulović failed to qualify Lebanon for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup|2018 World Cup]], he led them to their first-ever successful Asian Cup qualification campaign.<ref name="roosqual19" /> Between 29&nbsp;March 2016 to 9&nbsp;September 2018, Lebanon also recorded a 16-match unbeaten run,<ref name="kuwstreak">{{Cite news|url=https://menafn.com/1097557789/Kuwait-edge-win-over-Lebanon-to-break-record-unbeaten-run|title=Kuwait edge win over Lebanon to break record unbeaten run|date=10 November 2018|publisher=Mena FN|access-date=11 October 2018}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/football/socceroos/simon-hills-lebanon-scouting-report-ahead-of-socceroos-friendly/news-story/b984b8bcb79c4f57d81d5ac05ab3005f|title=Australia vs Lebanon international friendly preview, teams, Simon Hill analysis|last=Hill|first=Simon|date=19 November 2018|publisher=[[Fox Sports]]|access-date=17 December 2018}}</ref> winning eight and drawing eight games,<ref name="Morrison">{{Cite web|last=Morrison|first=Neil|title=Lebanon's series of 16 matches unbeaten|url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/unb-lebanon-16.html|access-date=15 January 2022|website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> and reached a then-best FIFA ranking of 77th in September 2018.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.dailytrust.com.ng/france-belgium-share-first-ever-joint-fifa-ranking.html|title=France, Belgium share first-ever joint FIFA ranking|date=20 September 2018|newspaper=[[Media Trust|Daily Trust]]|access-date=11 October 2018|archive-date=3 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190503193846/https://www.dailytrust.com.ng/france-belgium-share-first-ever-joint-fifa-ranking.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>

[[File:Lebanon vs Saudi Arabi 20191201 08.jpg|thumb|Lebanon during the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup|2019 Asian Cup]] group stage match against [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]]|alt=Hassan Maatouk and a Saudi player running while looking in the same direction]]
At the 2019 Asian Cup, Lebanon relied heavily on players from the [[Lebanese diaspora]], with nine members of the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup squads#Lebanon|23-man squad]] being [[List of Lebanon international footballers born outside Lebanon|born outside the country]].<ref name="TRTdiaspora" /> Drawn in [[2019 AFC Asian Cup Group E|Group E]], Lebanon opened the tournament with a 2–0 defeat against eventual champions Qatar;<ref name="AFC19QAT">{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-qatar-2-0-lebanon|title=Group E: Qatar 2–0 Lebanon|date=9 January 2019|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190504003928/http://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-qatar-2-0-lebanon|archive-date=4 May 2019|access-date=10 January 2020}}</ref> [[Ali Hamam]] had scored from a [[Corner kick|corner]] in the first half, though the goal was controversially disallowed for a [[Fouls and misconduct (association football)|foul]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.alaraby.co.uk/english/blog/2019/1/10/asian-cup-2019-qatar-routs-lebanon-in-dramatic-debut|title=Asian Cup 2019: Qatar routs Lebanon in dramatic debut|last=Levy|first=Uri|date=10 January 2019|publisher=[[The New Arab]]|access-date=13 January 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://thethao247.vn/72-afc-asian-2019-truc-tiep-qatar-vs-lebanon-23h00-ngay-9-1-d171910.html|title=Qatar nhọc nhằn đánh bại Lebanon ngày ra quân|last=Hùng|first=Minh|date=10 January 2019|publisher=Thể thao 247|language=vi|trans-title=Qatar struggled to defeat Lebanon on the first matchday|access-date=13 January 2019|archive-date=5 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230605010155/https://thethao247.vn/72-afc-asian-2019-truc-tiep-qatar-vs-lebanon-23h00-ngay-9-1-d171910.html|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Mikdadi |first=Bassil |date=13 May 2023 |title=2023 AFC Asian Cup: the full group stage review |url=https://www.arabnews.com/node/2302626/sport |access-date=14 May 2023 |publisher=[[Arab News]] |quote=A first half goal from Ali Hammam was controversially ruled out.}}</ref> A second 2–0 defeat, against Saudi Arabia, left Lebanon needing a four-goal victory over North Korea in their final group match to have a chance of progressing.<ref name="AFC19KSA">{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-lebanon-0-2-saudi-arabia|title=Group E: Lebanon 0–2 Saudi Arabia|date=12 January 2019|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190503160434/http://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-lebanon-0-2-saudi-arabia|archive-date=3 May 2019|access-date=10 January 2020}}</ref> Lebanon won 4–1 thanks to a brace from [[Hilal El-Helwe]], securing the country's first-ever Asian Cup victory.<ref name="AFC19PRK">{{Cite web|url=http://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-lebanon-4-1-dpr-korea|title=Group E: Lebanon 4–1 DPR Korea|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190503111914/https://www.the-afc.com/asiancup/news/group-e-lebanon-4-1-dpr-korea|archive-date=3 May 2019|access-date=10 January 2020}}</ref> However, they were eliminated on the fair play rule after finishing level on points and goal difference with Vietnam in the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup#Ranking of third-placed teams|third-place ranking]], having received two more [[Penalty card|yellow cards]].<ref name="AFC19PRK" />

[[Liviu Ciobotariu]] was appointed head coach for the joint qualification campaign the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2022 World Cup]] and [[2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2023 Asian Cup]].<ref name="Ciobotariu">{{Cite web|date=3 June 2019|script-title=ar:الروماني تشوبوتاريو سيتولى تدريب منتخب لبنان (رسمي)|trans-title=The Romanian Ciobotariu will take over the Lebanese national team (official)|url=https://www.france24.com/ar/20190603-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%85%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%8A-%D8%AA%D8%B4%D9%88%D8%A8%D9%88%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%8A%D9%88-%D8%B3%D9%8A%D8%AA%D9%88%D9%84%D9%89-%D8%AA%D8%AF%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%A8-%D9%85%D9%86%D8%AA%D8%AE%D8%A8-%D9%84%D8%A8%D9%86%D8%A7%D9%86-%D8%B1%D8%B3%D9%85%D9%8A|access-date=3 June 2019|publisher=[[France 24]]|language=ar}}</ref> After a mixed performance at the [[2019 WAFF Championship]],<ref name="GSAWAFF19">{{Cite web|url=https://globalsportsarchive.com/competition/soccer/asiacell-waff-championship-2019-iraq/group-stage/32914/|title=AsiaCell WAFF Championship 2019 Iraq – Results, fixtures, tables and stats|website=Global Sports Archive|access-date=29 April 2020}}</ref> Lebanon began the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round of qualification]] for the 2022 World Cup against South Korea, North Korea, Turkmenistan, and Sri Lanka.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/aponline/2019/07/17/world/middleeast/ap-soc-2022-wcup-asian-qualifying.html|title=North and South Korea to Meet in 2022 World Cup Qualifiers|date=17 July 2019|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=17 July 2019|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190717113356/https://www.nytimes.com/aponline/2019/07/17/world/middleeast/ap-soc-2022-wcup-asian-qualifying.html|archive-date=17 July 2019}}</ref> Following the postponement of fixtures due to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in Asia|COVID-19 pandemic]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/worldcup/news/update-on-upcoming-fifa-world-cup-qualifiers|title=Update on upcoming FIFA World Cup qualifiers in Asia|date=9 March 2020|publisher=[[FIFA]]|access-date=22 March 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.the-afc.com/competitions/afc-asian-cup/news/update-on-upcoming-fifa-world-cup-qualifiers|title=Update on upcoming FIFA World Cup qualifiers|date=9 March 2020|publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]|access-date=22 March 2020}}</ref> former captain Jamal Taha took charge in 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Mahfoud|first=Maroun|date=2020-06-17|title=Official: Jamal Taha is the new coach for the Lebanese national team|url=https://www.falebanon.com/official-jamal-taha-is-the-new-coach-for-the-lebanese-national-team/|access-date=2020-06-17|website=FA Lebanon}}</ref> North Korea's withdrawal from qualification in 2021 proved beneficial to Lebanon,<ref>{{Cite web|date=27 May 2021|script-title=ar:إلغاء نتائج كوريا الشمالية.. فرص لبنان بالتأهل تتضاعف|trans-title=Cancellation of North Korea results.. Lebanon's chances of qualifying double|url=http://lebanonfg.com/single/2685|access-date=2 June 2021|website=Lebanon FG|language=ar|archive-date=6 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230606015536/http://lebanonfg.com/single/2685|url-status=dead}}</ref> who advanced to the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|final round of World Cup qualification]] for the second time and qualified for the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023 Asian Cup]] for the third time.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Abou Diab |first=Rami |date=15 June 2021 |title=Historic day for Lebanon |url=https://www.falebanon.com/28263/ |access-date=15 June 2021 |website=FA Lebanon}}</ref>

Under [[Ivan Hašek]], Lebanon competed in the final round of 2022 World Cup qualification against Iran, South Korea, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq, and Syria. After earning five points from their opening four matches, including a 3–2 win over Syria,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 October 2021 |title=Lebanon hold off Syria for first victory |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/report_syria_v_lebanon.html |access-date=30 March 2022 |publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]}}</ref> Lebanon were considered an early surprise package of the qualifiers.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Duerden |first=John |date=14 October 2021 |title=Why Lebanon could just end up being the surprise package of the Asian Qualifiers to Qatar 2022 |url=https://www.arabnews.com/node/1948031/sport |access-date=30 March 2022 |publisher=[[Arab News]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=12 October 2021 |title=Five Takeaways from AFC Asian Qualifiers – Road to Qatar Matchday Four |url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/asian_qualifiers/news/five_takeaways_from_afc_asian_qualifiers_-_road_to_qatar_matchday_four_.html |access-date=30 March 2022 |publisher=[[Asian Football Confederation]]}}</ref> However, they earned only one point from their remaining six matches and finished bottom of the group.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 March 2022 |title=Iran Squanders Lebanon's Last Chance To Qualify For The World Cup |url=https://thenewsglory.com/iran-squanders-lebanons-last-chance-to-qualify-for-the-world-cup/ |access-date=30 March 2022 |website=The News Glory }}{{Dead link|date=August 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> The campaign was later featured in FIFA and Netflix's documentary series ''Captains''.<ref name="FIFA+" />

[[File:Qatar v Lebanon (16).jpg|thumb|Lebanon during the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023 Asian Cup]] group stage match against [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]]|alt=A Lebanese player surrounded by three Qatari defenders]]
In preparation for the 2023 Asian Cup, Lebanon finished runners-up at the [[2023 Intercontinental Cup (India)|2023 Intercontinental Cup]],<ref name="intercontinental23">{{Cite web |last=Rawat |first=Akhil |date=18 June 2023 |title=Champions!
